text.heading: Yar'Adua set to receive repentant militants
text.p: President Umaru Yar'Adua was ecstatic yesterday over a reported offer by Niger Delta Vigilante Movement (NDVM) and Niger Delta Patriotic Forces (NDPF) leader, Ateke Tom, to lay down his arms.

"I am highly delighted by this news that one of the militant leaders is prepared to accept the offer of amnesty by government. In fact, I hope that all militant leaders will do the same," the President said in Abuja while receiving his Sierra Leonean counterpart, Ernest Bai Koroma.
But Tom, through his lawyer Ikenna Enekweizu, denied offering to surrender in return for the amnesty promised by the President.

"There was never a time that my client, Ateke Tom, said he was going to surrender. He never said so. What we said is that Ateke Tom is ready to hand over his arms and those of his boys if the conditions for amnesty, when released, are acceptable to him," Enekweizu told The Nation on phone yesterday.
Reiterating that the military operation in the Niger Delta is aimed at ensuring peace in the region, Yar'Adua said the details of the amnesty would be released this week.

"We are not fighting, we are all Nigerians and what we are trying to do is to guarantee peace and security in the Niger Delta just like in any other part of Nigeria, so that people can live, work, carry on their normal duties without fear within the region, so that the region and the state can realise their great potentials for attracting foreign direct investments (FDIs) and boosting economic activities.

"Let me say that I am expecting in the next few days, certainly in the next few days, certainly within this week, for the committee on amnesty to complete all arrangements and make public (of course, I will personally make public) all the conditions, procedures and mechanisms for the amnesty. The amnesty centre that will be established, the procedures for granting the amnesty, documentation, camping, training, rehabilitation, getting jobs, for those who require education, sending them to school, all the terms will be made public.

"I will welcome any leader, in fact, I make bold to say that it will be a great pleasure for me to personally accept the first militant leader who takes advantage of this amnesty, to encourage others to do so; so that they can have confidence that we are sincere, honest in granting of this amnesty. We have no other interests than the interests of Nigerians to live in peace, and all those Nigerians and non-Nigerians alike who live within the Niger Delta states to live in peace and pursue their activities without fear. So, I welcome any leader and all his members who are ready to accept the offer of amnesty by government and I assure them that within the week, latest early next week, the terms will be made public."
In his remarks, President Bai Koroma, said his visit was meant to deepen existing relationship between Nigeria and Sierra Leone.
He also said that the issue of consolidating peace with the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) was discussed.

"I am very very delighted to be here, and we are here in furtherance of deepening relationship that exists between Nigeria and Sierra Leone. During my discussions with the President, we discussed issues that bordered on strengthening the cordial relationship that exists between the two countries. We discussed issues on consolidating peace within the ECOWAS sub-region, we discussed issues that will strengthen trade relations between the two countries and we shared a lot of experiences amongst us that will enhance the development and governance of our respective countries. We in Sierra Leone always look up to Nigeria as our big brother and we believe that President Yar'Adua being the current president of ECOWAS, we must come to give him support and hold regular consultation as expected of all of us, all of which is in furtherance of strengthening cooperation within the sub-region and also the ties that exist between Nigeria and Sierra Leone," he said.

Enekweizu said for the amnesty conditions to be acceptable to Ateke Tom, "they must embody a solution to the perennial and fundamental issue of the Niger Delta question, resource control, and fair treatment for Niger Delta people by the Federal Government."
He added: "There must be an indication that this government is truly committed to ending the Niger Delta crisis and finding an amicable solution to it".

Enekweizu said he was not opposed to Yar'Adua’s comments on the reported decision of Ateke Tom to surrender, adding: "I have always maintained that at the end of every fight, people gather at the roundtable to talk".
The Rivers State Government yesterday described as diversionary Ateke Tom’s reported decision to surrender his arms.

A statement by the Acting Chief Press Secretary to Governor Rotimi Amaechi, Mr Blessing Wikina, said the militant leader should not be taken seriously in view of his several antics in the past which led to severe consequences for his people.
"Peace cannot be achieved by proxy, it is either he is committed to the process or he is not", the government said, adding: "He is insincere and should not be listened to".

The statement added that if Tom was serious about his commitment to peace in the Niger Delta, and the Federal Government’s amnesty offer, he should have come out of hiding with his loyalists and guns, rather than speaking through a proxy.

"This is not the first time Ateke Tom has deceived the people with the promise to contribute to the peace process in Okrika and we believe that it is a ploy to re-enter Okrika to unleash mayhem", the statement said.
Wikina urged the people to discountenance Tom’s claim as he had not shown remorse for the self-confessed killings he masterminded in the state.

The statement added: "While the Rivers State Government welcomes every genuine effort at seeking the Federal Government’s amnesty, we believe that such persons must show genuine and remorseful commitment to the process".
